raw_consumer_statement:
I reported fraud and misrepresentation related to a {$4400.00} charge from XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX as XX/XX/XXXX by phone. At that time, I was told I could use the timeshare in XX/XX/XXXX but was later denied access because I had not completed a required orientation, which was not available until XX/XX/XXXX. This condition was never disclosed at the time of purchase. I also paid {$440.00} toward the charge before learning I would be denied service. 

In response to the issue, I filed a police report and a complaint with the Federal Trade Commission, both of which I uploaded to AMEX, giving the appearance that AMEX was investigating the matter. Numerous AMEX representatives spent hours with me over several months addressing my concerns. 

However, AMEX closed my dispute on XX/XX/XXXX, falsely stating I reported the issue more than 60 days after the statement date, which is incorrectI first raised the issue in XX/XX/XXXX. Subsequently, AMEX canceled my card, appears to have charged off the balance, and reported negative credit information to credit bureaus. 

AMEX also informed me that because I provided my account number to the service establishment ( XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ), the charges were considered authorized and thus not eligible for fraud protection. 

This dispute originated from deceptive sales tactics used during a promotional offer. I was persuaded to accept a XXXX getaway deal that required attending a pressured XXXX sales orientation designed to upsell timeshare ownershipan environment I now recognize as coercive and misrepresentative. 

Throughout this unresolved dispute, I have also been subjected to near-daily robocalls from AMEX, XXXX ongoing harassment.
